title: Warmup: multiplying binomials intro
description: Acquire your binomial multiplication skills through this structured set of problems.
topic_path: Module 1: Relationships between quantities and reasoning with equations and their graphs

hints:
There are two really good ways to think about this problem:

1. Area models
2. Applying distributive property twice

**Distributive property twice**

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{(x+3)}(x+4)
\\\\
=&\blueD{(x+3)}x+\blueD{(x+3)}4
\\\\
=&\blueD x\cdot x+\blueD 3\cdot x+\blueD x\cdot 4+\blueD 3\cdot 4
\\\\
=&x^2+3x+4x+12
\\\\
=&x^2+7x+12
\end{align}$

Either way we look at it, we get the same answer:

$(x+3)(x+4)=x^2+7x+12$

There are two really good ways to think about this problem:

1. Area models
2. Applying distributive property twice

**Area models**

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5e0ce6318667573ebdf33ec3b76f54716da9b937)

$\begin{align}
&(x-2)(x+5)
\\\\
=&\blueD{x^2}+(\maroonC{-2x})+\greenC{5x}+(\goldC{-10})
\\\\
=&x^2+3x-10
\end{align}$

*This method is very useful even though it's a little weird to have negative values for area. Consider it as area that's subtracted from the resulting product.*

**Distributive property twice**

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{(x-2)}(x+5)
\\\\
=&\blueD{(x-2)}x+\blueD{(x-2)}5
\\\\
=&\blueD x\cdot x\blueD {-2}\cdot x+\blueD x\cdot 5\blueD{-2}\cdot 5
\\\\
=&x^2-2x+5x-10
\\\\
=&x^2+3x-10
\end{align}$

Either way we look at it, we get the same answer:

$(x-2)(x+5)=x^2+3x-10$

There are two really good ways to think about this problem:

1. Area models
2. Applying distributive property twice

**Area models**

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e5e1678f565ad8b203d01a6fc16a14f5d2fe99e7)

$\begin{align}
&(x+1)(x-6)
\\\\
=&\blueD{x^2}+\maroonC{x}+(\greenC{-6x})+(\goldC{-6})
\\\\
=&x^2-5x-6
\end{align}$

*This method is very useful even though it's a little weird to have negative values for area. Consider it as area that's subtracted from the resulting product.*

**Distributive property twice**

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{(x+1)}(x-6)
\\\\
=&\blueD{(x+1)}x+\blueD{(x+1)}(-6)
\\\\
=&\blueD x\cdot x+\blueD {1}\cdot x+\blueD x(-6)+\blueD{1}(-6)
\\\\
=&x^2+x-6x-6
\\\\
=&x^2-5x-6
\end{align}$

Either way we look at it, we get the same answer:

$(x+1)(x-6)=x^2-5x-6$

There are two really good ways to think about this problem:

1. Area models
2. Applying distributive property twice

**Area models**

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/39600f17fa95bd76d8e498b403491a21a682258b)

$\begin{align}
&(x-3)(x-5)
\\\\
=&\blueD{x^2}+(\maroonC{-3x})+(\greenC{-5x})+\goldC{15}
\\\\
=&x^2-8x+15
\end{align}$

*This method is very useful even though it's a little weird to have negative values for area. Consider it as area that's subtracted from the resulting product.*

**Distributive property twice**

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{(x-3)}(x-5)
\\\\
=&\blueD{(x-3)}x+\blueD{(x-3)}(-5)
\\\\
=&\blueD x\cdot x\blueD {-3}\cdot x+\blueD x(-5)\blueD{-3}(-5)
\\\\
=&x^2-3x-5x+15
\\\\
=&x^2-8x+15
\end{align}$

Either way we look at it, we get the same answer:

$(x-3)(x-5)=x^2-8x+15$
